problem_statement.Title: Pytest assertion rewrite crashes with TypeError when module's first expression is a numeric literal
problem_statement.Description: When a test module begins with a numeric literal (or other non-string Constant) as its first top-level expression, pytest's assertion rewrite raises TypeError: "argument of type 'int' is not iterable" during collection.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect docstring detection in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py : AssertionRewriter.run() treats any ast.Constant as a module docstring and assigns doc = item.value.value unconditionally. This assumes the Constant's value is a string when it may be an int (or other non-string), so a numeric first expression gets mistaken for a docstring. The correct relationship is: only treat item.value.value as a docstring when isinstance(item.value.value, (str, bytes)).

2) Fragile membership test in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py : AssertionRewriter.is_rewrite_disabled assumes its argument is a str and does "PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" in docstring, which raises TypeError if docstring is an int. The method encodes the incorrect assumption that callers always pass a str; it should instead be defensive and only perform the membership test for str (and for bytes use the bytes marker b"PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE"), otherwise return False. Both issues together cause the TypeError when the module's first statement is a numeric literal.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python file (e.g. test_bad.py) with the following contents:
def test_dummy():
assert True
Run pytest on that file (e.g. `pytest test_bad.py`).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should collect and run the test(s) in the module normally. A numeric literal as the first top-level expression must not be treated as a docstring and must not cause a TypeError during assertion rewriting; collection should complete and tests should be executed.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Collection fails with a TypeError during assertion rewrite: "TypeError: argument of type 'int' is not iterable" (raised when the rewrite code checks for the "PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" marker on a non-string value).

problem_statement.Title: pmxbot.logging module imported twice under import-mode=importlib causing Logger.store not to be set
problem_statement.Description: After switching to PEP 420 namespace packages and pytest import-mode=importlib, core.initialize() sets a Logger.store attribute but tests see a Logger class with no store attribute. Investigation shows two distinct pmxbot.logging module objects exist at runtime, so the class attribute mutation is applied to one module object but not the other.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) core.initialize assigns the runtime store onto the Logger class object (location: core.initialize in pmxbot/core.py). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the pmxbot.logging module (and its Logger class) exists as a single, unique module/class object process-wide. The assignment (pmxbot.logging.Logger.store = <store>) only affects the Logger class object in that particular module object.

2) pmxbot/logging.py defines a Logger class and relies on a class attribute named 'store' as the canonical process-wide logging store (location: pmxbot/logging.py, Logger class). This encodes the incorrect assumption that attaching global state to a module-level class attribute will be visible to every importer of pmxbot.logging.

3) The test/runtime environment is using pytest import-mode=importlib and PEP 420 namespace packages (pytest.ini / test configuration referenced in the report). That configuration has caused pmxbot.logging to be loaded into two distinct module objects (evidence: in PDB sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging returned False and the two module objects have the same file path but are different identities). This violates the assumption in (1) and (2) and explains why the class attribute set by core.initialize is not visible to the test's copy of the module.

4) tests/unit/test_commands.py (setup_class) calls core.initialize(config) and then immediately uses logging.Logger.store.message(...). The test assumes core.initialize's mutation is visible to the logging.Logger referenced by the test module. The observed AttributeError ('type object "Logger" has no attribute "store"') is the direct symptom caused by findings 1–3.
StepsToReproduce: Checkout the pmxbot tree at the described commit (pmxbot/pmxbot@7f189ad).
Ensure pytest is configured with import-mode=importlib (as in the report's pytest.ini).
Run the unit tests (for example: pytest tests/unit/test_commands.py or pytest on the repository).
Observe failure in tests/unit/test_commands.py setup_class when calling logging.Logger.store.message(...).
In a failing run, drop to a debugger and evaluate: logging, sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'], and (sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging) to see two distinct module objects.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After core.initialize(config) completes, the test's reference to pmxbot.logging.Logger.store exists and can be used (e.g., logging.Logger.store.message(...) succeeds). There is a single effective process-wide logging store observable by all importers of pmxbot.logging during the test run.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: core.initialize executes and appears to set pmxbot.logging.Logger.store, but tests get AttributeError: type object 'Logger' has no attribute 'store' when calling logging.Logger.store.message(...). Debugging shows two distinct module objects for pmxbot.logging (sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging evaluates to False), so the mutation applied by initialize is not visible to the module object used by the tests.

problem_statement.Title: Assertion rewriter does not unroll comprehensions passed to all()/any(), producing unhelpful failure messages
problem_statement.Description: When an assertion uses all(...) or any(...) with a generator expression or list comprehension, pytest's assertion rewriter prints the comprehension as an opaque value (a generator object or a list of booleans) instead of giving a per-element failure explanation. As a result failures lack the detailed 'where ...' message that the equivalent explicit for-loop/assert produces.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) AssertionRewriter lacks special-casing for builtin calls to 'all' and 'any' in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py: the call visitors (visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy) do not detect when the single positional argument is a comprehension (ast.GeneratorExp or ast.ListComp). They let the generic call-handling path run instead.  
2) The generic/fallback handling treats comprehension arguments as opaque expressions: generic_visit assigns the comprehension result to a temporary variable and renders its representation (a generator object repr for generator expressions, or saferepr() of the list for list comprehensions) rather than expanding the comprehension into per-element expressions that the rewriter can explain.  
3) The rewriter therefore assumes a comprehension is a single value rather than a sequence of element-expressions that can be individually rewritten and explained; this incorrect assumption is encoded in the visitor fallback logic in rewrite.py and prevents producing per-element 'where ...' explanations.  
4) The current design does not preserve the necessary relationship between comprehension evaluation and all()/any() short-circuit semantics: the rewriter would need to unroll the comprehension into a lightweight loop (nested For/If AST nodes for multiple generators and comprehension .ifs) that preserves short-circuiting and name bindings instead of materializing the entire iterable. The absence of this relationship in rewrite.py is a root cause (both visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy must handle it).  
5) As currently implemented, the rewriter's behavior differs between writing an explicit for-loop with an assert (which produces a detailed element-level explanation) and using all()/any() with a comprehension (which produces only an opaque repr). This behavioral mismatch is encoded in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py's handling of call arguments and generic_visit.
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on that file (e.g. pytest test_all_any.py -q).
Observe the failure output for each test variant.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an assertion uses all(...) or any(...) with a comprehension, pytest should produce a failure message that identifies the failing element and shows the inner predicate expression and values (the same kind of 'where ...' / intermediate-value explanation produced by an explicit for-loop/assert). Short-circuit semantics should be preserved (the report should reflect the first element that determines the result).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For the generator-expression form: pytest reports only a generic False and shows the generator object (e.g. 'all(<generator object ...>)') with no per-element detail. For the list-comprehension form: pytest prints a list of booleans but does not identify which element/predicate failed in a readable intermediate-value explanation. The explicit for-loop/assert form does produce a meaningful failure message (e.g. 'where False = is_even(1)').

problem_statement.Title: pytest --fixtures and --fixtures-per-test do not show fixture scope
problem_statement.Description: When listing fixtures with `pytest --fixtures` or `pytest --fixtures-per-test`, the fixture scope (function/module/class/session/package) is not included in the displayed output. Users must consult docs or source to learn a fixture's scope.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Omission of fixture.scope in fixtures-per-test display (src/_pytest/python.py, function _show_fixtures_per_test, nested write_fixture): the code formats and prints the fixture name and, in verbose mode, the defining location, but never uses FixtureDef.scope. The desired relationship is to include scope in the display; e.g. non-verbose: "{argname} ({scope})" and verbose: "{argname} ({scope}) -- {path}:{line}" (format string: "{} ({}) -- {}").

2) Omission of fixture.scope in overall fixtures display (src/_pytest/python.py, function _showfixtures_main): this code also formats and prints only the fixture name and, when verbose, the location, but omits the fixture scope. The correct relationship is identical to (1): include scope as "{argname} ({scope})" (non-verbose) or "{argname} ({scope}) -- {path}:{line}" (verbose).

3) Tests assert the old output format without scopes (testing/python/show_fixtures_per_test.py): test expectations are encoded assuming fixture names are shown without scopes; those assertions will not match output once scope is included. The encoded assumption is that the textual fixtures output excludes scope, and must be updated to expect the scope string (for example, replacing occurrences like "arg1" with "arg1 (function)" in assertions).

4) The FixtureDef.scope attribute exists and provides the required value (src/_pytest/fixtures.py, class FixtureDef): the implementation has access to a fixture's scope but the display code assumes/encodes that the scope should not be shown. This is an incorrect assumption encoded in the display functions referenced above.
StepsToReproduce: Create a small test module with one or more fixtures (e.g. def my_fixture(): ...).
Run `pytest --fixtures` and observe the fixture listing output.
Run `pytest --fixtures-per-test` (or `pytest -v --fixtures-per-test`) and observe the per-test fixture listing output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The fixtures listing should include each fixture's scope in the human-readable output. For example, non-verbose lines should appear as "my_fixture (function)" and verbose lines should appear as "my_fixture (function) -- path/to/file.py:line". Scopes should reflect the fixture's actual scope value (function/module/class/session/package).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Fixture listings show the fixture name (and, in verbose mode, the defining file/location) but do not show the fixture's scope. For example: "my_fixture" (non-verbose) or "my_fixture -- path/to/file.py:line" (verbose) — no scope is printed.

problem_statement.Title: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT omits logger name (record.name); default log lines lack module name
problem_statement.Description: Pytest's default logging format does not include the logger/module name (LogRecord.name). Users expect the module name to appear in default log messages; instead only filename, lineno, level and message are present. The default constant is defined in one location and tests assert the old layout.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Hardcoded DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT in src/_pytest/logging.py encodes an assumption that the filename and lineno are sufficient and omits the logger name. Location: src/_pytest/logging.py. Current value: "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s". Correct relationship/value (per investigation): the format should include %(name)s (logger/module name). Example suggested value: "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s".

2) Tests assert the old literal format and exact formatted output, so they encode the old assumption about DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT. Location: testing/logging/test_formatter.py. Current test format string: "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s". Current expected outputs in the test (examples):
   - colored case: "dummypath                   10 \x1b[32mINFO    \x1b[0m Test Message"
   - non-colored case: "dummypath                   10 INFO     Test Message"
   The test expectations must be updated to match any new default format (for example, new expected outputs per investigation):
   - colored case: "\x1b[32mINFO    \x1b[0m dummy:dummypath:10 Test Message"
   - non-colored case: "INFO     dummy:dummypath:10 Test Message".

3) DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is the single authoritative default used by the LoggingPlugin and as the --log-format default (so the constant controls behavior everywhere). Location: src/_pytest/logging.py (used as default for CLI/file logging and passed to logging.Formatter). The implicit assumption that changing this constant has only local effect is incorrect — it is the global default.
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest (no custom --log-format) with a test that emits logging output from different modules.
Observe the default log lines produced by pytest (for example, DEBUG logs from django.db.backends and multipart).
Compare the lines to confirm the logger/module name is not present and only filename, lineno, level and message appear.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior:
Default pytest log lines include the logger/module name so output shows which module produced the message. Example expected default output (user-visible):
DEBUG    django.db.backends:utils.py:114 (0.000) SELECT "app_url"."id", "app_url"."created", "app_url"."url" FROM "app_url" WHERE "app_url"."id" = 2; args=(2,)
DEBUG    multipart.multipart:multipart.py:604 Calling on_field_start with no data
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Default pytest log lines omit the logger/module name and only show filename, lineno, level and message. Example current output (user-visible):

multipart.py               604 DEBUG    Calling on_field_start with no data

problem_statement.Title: str() of pytest.raises context (ExceptionInfo) shows file:lineno and only first line of exception message (truncates multiline messages)
problem_statement.Description: When using the pytest.raises(...) context manager the object bound with 'as e' is an ExceptionInfo-like object whose str() representation prints a short file:lineno: <exception-first-line> form instead of the exception instance's full message. Multiline exception messages are therefore truncated and differ from the result of str(exception_instance) in a normal except block.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The object returned by pytest.raises(...) is an ExceptionInfo wrapper, not the raw exception instance (RaisesContext in src/_pytest/python_api.py returns an ExceptionInfo). The user assumption that str(excinfo) should equal str(exception_instance) is therefore incorrect.  
2) ExceptionInfo.__str__ (src/_pytest/_code/code.py) constructs a ReprFileLocation using the last traceback entry and exconly(), then returns str(loc). Concretely: it does something equivalent to loc = ReprFileLocation(entry.path, entry.lineno + 1, self.exconly()); return str(loc). This produces a file:lineno: message representation rather than the exception value's string.  
3) The ReprFileLocation/terminal formatting logic intentionally truncates the provided message at the first newline (it finds msg.find("\n") and uses only the first line for the single-line display). Because ExceptionInfo.__str__ uses exconly() as the message fed to ReprFileLocation, multiline exception messages (e.g. "A\nB\nC") are reduced to the first line ("A") in the final str(ExceptionInfo) output.  
4) Tests and internal code encode and assert the current short representation: e.g. testing/code/test_excinfo.py contains expectations that str(excinfo) begins with the filename and contains the exception type (this demonstrates code/tests rely on the existing file:lineno: first-line formatting), so changing the str() semantics affects those locations.
StepsToReproduce: Create and run this try/except snippet in plain Python:
try:
raise LookupError("A\nB\nC")
except LookupError as e:
print(str(e))
Observe that the output is the full multiline message (A\nB\nC).
Create and run this pytest snippet (pytest 4.6.2 style):
import pytest
with pytest.raises(LookupError) as e:
raise LookupError("A\nB\nC")
print(str(e))
Observe that the output is a short file:lineno: LookupError: A (only the first line) instead of the full multiline message.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible: printing the context object bound by 'with pytest.raises(...) as e' should present the full exception message (including all newlines) in the same way that str(exception_instance) does inside a normal except block, so multiline messages are not truncated and are immediately visible via str(e).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: User-visible: printing the pytest.raises context variable (str(e)) shows a short file:lineno: <exception-first-line> form (for example "<console>:3: LookupError: A"). Multiline exception messages are truncated to the first line; to see the full message the user must use str(e.value).

> Any chance the behavior could be changed to eliminate this gotcha?

What do you suggest?

Proxying through to the exceptions `__str__`?
Hi @fiendish,

Indeed this is a bit confusing.

Currently `ExceptionInfo` objects (which is `pytest.raises` returns to the context manager) implements `__str__` like this:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/9f8b566ea976df3a3ea16f74b56dd6d4909b84ee/src/_pytest/_code/code.py#L537-L542

I don't see much use for this, I would rather it didn't implement `__str__` at all and let `__repr__` take over, which would show something like:

```
<ExceptionInfo LookupError tb=10>
```

Which makes it more obvious that this is not what the user intended with `str(e)` probably.

So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Thoughts?

Also, @fiendish which Python version are you using?
> So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Makes sense to me.


Python 3.7.3

My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.
> My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.

I understand, but I think it is better to be explicit here, because users might use `print(e)` to see what `e` is, assume it is the exception value, and then get confused later when it actually isn't (an `isinstance` check or accessing `e.args`).
+1 for deleting the current `__str__` implementation
-1 for proxying it to the underlying `e.value`

the `ExceptionInfo` object is not the exception and anything that makes it look more like the exception is just going to add to the confusion

problem_statement.Title: Assertion diff shows byte values as integer ordinals (e.g. 52) when comparing bytes
problem_statement.Description: When pytest formats assertion failure diffs for bytes objects, bytes are classified as sequences of ints (not text). That causes the sequence-diff path to report per-element integer ordinals (e.g. 52 for b'4') instead of showing the readable byte/string representation.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) istext() wrongly excludes bytes: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py::istext currently returns isinstance(x, str). This classification causes bytes to be treated as generic sequences rather than as text-like objects. 2) assertrepr_compare dispatches on istext/issequence/isiterable: when two operands are not considered text, the code uses the sequence comparison path (_compare_eq_sequence) which reports an extra element using saferepr(right[len_left]). For bytes this yields integer elements (e.g. '52'). Location: src/_pytest/assertion/util.py. 3) _diff_text already contains logic to render bytes as readable escaped text (escape_for_readable_diff) but it is only used when istext(left) and istext(right) are True. Thus the correct representation (readable bytes like b'42' / '42') is available in code but never reached for bytes because of the istext classification. Correct relationship/values: istext should return True for both str and bytes (i.e. isinstance(x, (str, bytes))) so that assertrepr_compare will call _diff_text for bytes-vs-bytes and produce readable byte diffs. (Note: bytearray was not changed; handling bytearray would require also updating _diff_text.)
StepsToReproduce: Create a test that compares two bytes with different lengths, e.g.:
def test_b():
assert b'' == b'42'
Run pytest and observe the assertion failure message.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When two bytes objects are compared in an assertion, the failure message shows a readable, escaped byte/string diff (characters like '4'/'42' in a human-readable form) rather than integer ordinals for each byte.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The failure message treats bytes as sequences of integers and prints integer ordinals for extra bytes (e.g. 'Right contains more items, first extra item: 52' where 52 is ord('4')).

hints_text: hmmm yes, this ~kinda makes sense as `bytes` objects are sequences of integers -- we should maybe just omit the "contains more items" messaging for bytes objects?

problem_statement.Title: pytest junitxml reporter omits testsuite 'hostname' and 'timestamp' attributes
problem_statement.Description: When pytest generates a JUnit XML report (via the junitxml reporter / --junitxml), the <testsuite> element is emitted without hostname and timestamp attributes that are normally present in JUnit-style reports.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) The code path that constructs the <testsuite> element is a single location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py (LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish()). That call to Junit.testsuite(...) never includes timestamp or hostname attributes, so they are never emitted. 2) The reporter does not compute a timestamp value; the investigation shows the correct value should be computed from the suite start time as: time.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S", time.localtime(self.suite_start_time)) (produces e.g. 2019-04-22T10:32:27). 3) The reporter does not compute a hostname value; the investigation shows the correct value should be the OS hostname obtained via socket.gethostname(). 4) The module currently lacks the socket import/usage to obtain the hostname and therefore does not supply hostname as an attribute when creating the testsuite element. 5) Tests in testing/test_junitxml.py currently assert other testsuite attributes but do not assert presence of timestamp/hostname, so the missing attributes were not detected by existing tests (coverage gap).
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on any test suite while requesting a JUnit XML output: pytest --junitxml=results.xml
Open the generated results.xml and inspect the <testsuite> element at the top-level
Observe that the testsuite element contains attributes like name, tests, failures, errors, skipped, time but does not contain timestamp or hostname
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element includes a timestamp attribute in the form YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS and a hostname attribute containing the machine's hostname (e.g. hostname="Anass-MacBook-Pro.local").
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element lacks timestamp and hostname attributes (only contains attributes such as name, tests, failures, errors, skipped, time).

problem_statement.Title: pytest: --collect-only has no one-character shortcut (feature request)
problem_statement.Description: The CLI option --collect-only (registered as --collectonly / --collect-only) has no single-character shortcut. Users frequently want a shorter alias for this long flag when invoking pytest interactively.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing short-option registration for collect-only: src/_pytest/main.py registers the option only with the long forms ("--collectonly", "--collect-only") via group.addoption(...). The registration does not include any single-character alias. The correct registration form should include a one-character short option (example: "-C") so that both "-C" and "--collect-only" are accepted. Concretely, replace the current call
   group.addoption("--collectonly", "--collect-only", action="store_true", help=...)
   with
   group._addoption("-C", "--collectonly", "--collect-only", action="store_true", help=...)
   (file: src/_pytest/main.py).

2) OptionGroup addoption API semantics prevent adding lowercase short options unless the alternate API is used: src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py encodes the rule in OptionGroup._addoption_instance that when shortupper=False (the path used by group.addoption) any short option with a lowercase letter is rejected. This is the reason group._addoption (which sets shortupper=True) must be used to register a short option when required. (file: src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py)

3) Short-letter collision constraint: many single-letter short flags are already defined across the codebase (examples include -v, -q, -s, -x, -k, -m, -c). These are registered in various modules such as src/_pytest/terminal.py and src/_pytest/capture.py. Because lowercase letters are largely taken, an uppercase letter is a safer choice; the investigation found "-C" (uppercase C) is not currently used and is mnemonic for "Collect". (multiple files; see registration sites such as src/_pytest/terminal.py, src/_pytest/capture.py for existing short flags).

4) Test coverage must be added to assert equivalence: tests in the repository currently exercise --collect-only (many test files call runpytest("--collect-only")). There is no test asserting a short alias exists; to prevent regressions a new test should be added (example location: testing/test_collection.py) that asserts running pytest with the short flag (e.g. "-C") behaves identically to the long flag ("--collect-only").

Run: pytest --collect-only and observe collection output (e.g. "collected 2 items").
Run: pytest -C and observe that the option is not recognized (no shortcut), or that pytest fails to accept -C (unrecognized option).

problem_statement.Title: INTERNALERROR when an object's __repr__ (or exception formatting) raises
problem_statement.Description: When pytest attempts to format an object whose __repr__ (or whose exception's repr/str) raises, saferepr's exception-formatting path itself can raise and produce an INTERNALERROR. The failure occurs while building a safe string for the exception raised during repr, causing pytest to surface an INTERNALERROR instead of a safe representation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. _try_repr_or_str in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py assumes that, after repr(obj) fails, formatting a fallback with obj (which invokes str(obj)) will not raise. Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_try_repr_or_str. Current behavior: on repr() failure it returns '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) which can call str(obj) and propagate another exception. Correct relationship: the function must never propagate exceptions (except KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit); if repr(obj) fails then attempts to obtain information must be guarded and, if they fail, a safe fallback (for example the failure exception's type name or the object's type name) should be returned.

2. _format_repr_exception in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py retries formatting the failure without protecting the second attempt. Location: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_format_repr_exception. Current behavior: it calls _try_repr_or_str(exc) in a try block; if that raises it catches it and then immediately calls _try_repr_or_str(exc) again inside format(...) without an additional guard. Correct relationship: if the first attempt to render the exception fails, any subsequent attempt to render information about that failure must be protected; if that also fails, the function must fall back to a safe string (e.g., the failure exception type name) so that _format_repr_exception never raises (except KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit).

3. Consequence: combination of (1) and (2) allows repeated failures when formatting a broken object's repr/str (for example code with bare 'raise' inside __repr__ or __getattribute__), causing saferepr to propagate the failure and pytest to show an INTERNALERROR instead of a safe diagnostic.
StepsToReproduce: Create a file test_pytest.py with the following contents:
class SomeClass:
def __getattribute__(self, attr):
raise
def __repr__(self):
raise
def test():
SomeClass().attr
Run pytest on that file (e.g. `pytest test_pytest.py`).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should display a test failure trace or a safe, non-raising representation for the problematic object/exception. Saferepr should not raise an unhandled exception while trying to produce a readable representation; at worst it should include the exception type name or a clear 'unpresentable exception' marker in the output (and always re-raise KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises an INTERNALERROR: during formatting of the test report the saferepr code attempts to format the exception raised by the object's __repr__, but this formatting path itself raises (e.g., RuntimeError: No active exception to reraise), producing an INTERNALERROR stack trace instead of a safe object representation.

problem_statement.Title: Traceback file paths relativized against current cwd instead of pytest invocation_dir (fixtures that chdir() break editor links)
problem_statement.Description: When a test or fixture changes the process working directory (os.chdir or monkeypatch.chdir), pytest formats file:line locations in failure tracebacks relative to the new cwd. This causes displayed paths like "../test_path_error.py:29" instead of remaining relative to the directory pytest was invoked from (invocation_dir). Editors/IDEs that resolve displayed paths using the original project directory cannot jump to the error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect base for path relativization encoded in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (FormattedExcinfo._makepath / ExceptionInfo.getrepr): file paths are shortened using py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) which uses the process current working directory (cwd). This makes displayed paths depend on any runtime chdir().

2) Caller sites that format test failure/exceptions do not provide pytest's stable invocation directory as a base. Specifically:
   - src/_pytest/nodes.py: Node._repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...) but does not pass Config.invocation_dir as the base; as a result, failure formatting can be influenced by a changed cwd.
   - src/_pytest/config/__init__.py: Config.notify_exception calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without passing invocation_dir as the base for path shortening.
   - src/_pytest/terminal.py: TerminalReporter.pytest_keyboard_interrupt (and places that memoize exception representations for later printing) calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without passing invocation_dir.

3) Missing API to request a specific base path: ExceptionInfo.getrepr / FormattedExcinfo did not accept a caller-supplied 'root' or 'base' path to use for bestrelpath; therefore formatting always fell back to the process cwd when abspath=False.

Correct relationships/values to preserve:
- When a caller has access to pytest's invocation directory (Config.invocation_dir, a py.path.local), that path should be used as the base for computing relative file paths: use invocation_dir.bestrelpath(path) instead of py.path.local().bestrelpath(path).
- The behavior for abspath=True must remain unchanged (absolute paths should still be returned).
- The new parameter must be optional (default None) to preserve existing behavior where invocation_dir is not available.
StepsToReproduce: Create a fixture that changes the current working directory (e.g., os.chdir('ddd') or monkeypatch.chdir('ddd')).
Create a test that uses the fixture and fails, e.g., assert False.
Run pytest from a project directory (the invocation directory) that contains the test file.

Observe the failure output: the test file path in the traceback is shown relative to the new cwd (e.g., "../test_path_error.py:29") instead of relative to the invocation directory ("test_path_error.py:29").
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Failure tracebacks display file:line locations relative to the directory pytest was invoked from (invocation_dir). Changing the cwd inside tests or fixtures should not change the base used to shorten displayed paths, so editors/IDEs can resolve the displayed paths to the original files.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Failure tracebacks display file:line locations relative to the current process working directory at formatting time. If a fixture changed cwd, displayed paths become relative to that directory (e.g., "../test_path_error.py:29"), preventing editors from locating the original file at the given location.

problem_statement.Title: skipif/xfail string conditions cached by expression only — ignores module globals
problem_statement.Description: Pytest caches the result of evaluating string conditions passed to @pytest.mark.skipif/@pytest.mark.xfail using only the expression string as the cache key. The evaluation actually depends on the test item's module globals, so reusing a cached result for the same expression across modules can produce incorrect skip/xfail decisions.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect caching key in src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py: the cache stored in config._store under evalcache_key maps expression string -> evaluation result (evalcache[expr] = eval(exprcode, d)), but the cache lookup ignores the evaluation globals 'd'. Location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, function cached_eval and its use.  
2) MarkEvaluator uses item-specific globals when evaluating string expressions (d returned by MarkEvaluator._getglobals includes the item's module globals via item.obj.__globals__), but MarkEvaluator._istrue calls cached_eval(self.item.config, expr, d) which reuses cached results keyed only by expr. Location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, method MarkEvaluator._istrue and method _getglobals.  
3) Wrong assumption: expression string alone uniquely determines the boolean result. In fact the correct relationship is result = eval(compile(expr, mode='eval'), d), where 'd' is the item/module globals; therefore results are dependent on both expr and the evaluation globals. The current cache incorrectly treats result as a function solely of expr, causing cross-module contamination of results.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: test_should_skip is reported as skipped; test_should_not_skip is executed (and fails in this example) — i.e., skipif string expressions are evaluated in each test's module/global context, so different modules with different globals can produce different results for the same expression string.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Both tests are skipped because the evaluation result for the expression string was cached from the first module and reused for the second, ignoring the second module's different globals.

hints_text:
> I think the most appropriate fix is to simply remove the caching, which I don't think is necessary really, and inline cached_eval into MarkEvaluator._istrue.

I agree:

* While it might have some performance impact with very large test suites which use marks with eval, the simple workaround is to not use the eval feature on those, which is more predictable anyway.
* I don't see a clean way to turn "globals" in some kind of cache key without having some performance impact and/or adverse effects.

So 👍 from me to simply removing this caching. 
As globals are dynamic, i would propose to drop the cache as well, we should investigate reinstating a cache later on

problem_statement.Title: pytest -rs --runxfail causes skip location to point to skipping.py instead of test item
problem_statement.Description: When tests are skipped via @pytest.mark.skip/skipif, the reported location should point to the test item (file:line). With --runxfail enabled, pytest prints the skip location as inside src/_pytest/skipping.py (where skip() is raised) instead of the test file/line.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Control-flow short-circuit in pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py): an early branch 'elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass' is part of an if/elif/... chain that prevents the later skip-location adjustment from running when runxfail is True. Location: src/_pytest/skipping.py, function pytest_runtest_makereport.

2) The skip-location adjustment is implemented as the final 'elif' in that chain and therefore is not executed when runxfail is True. The adjustment rewrites rep.longrepr (a (filename, lineno, reason) tuple) to use item.reportinfo() so the skip is reported at the test item location. The required relationship that must hold is: rep.longrepr should be (str(filename), line+1, reason) where (filename, line, ...) = item.reportinfo(), and this rewrite must run whenever the skip originates from a skip-mark (item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True) is True) AND rep.skipped is True AND type(rep.longrepr) is tuple. This rewrite currently does not run under --runxfail due to the elif short-circuit. Location: src/_pytest/skipping.py, same function.

3) No other code locations were found that replicate this exact control-flow problem; runxfail is only short-circuiting this chain in src/_pytest/skipping.py.
StepsToReproduce:
Run pytest normally with the summary of skipped tests: pytest -rs
  - Observe output: SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip

Run pytest with --runxfail added: pytest -rs --runxfail
  - Observe output: SKIPPED [1] src/_pytest/skipping.py:238: unconditional skip (location points into pytest internals)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a test is skipped by @pytest.mark.skip/skipif, the skip summary line should point to the test item's file and line (the item definition), e.g. 'SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip', regardless of whether --runxfail is enabled.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: With --runxfail enabled, the skip summary line shows the location where the skip() exception was raised inside pytest internals (src/_pytest/skipping.py) instead of the test file and line, e.g. 'SKIPPED [1] src/_pytest/skipping.py:238: unconditional skip'.

problem_statement.Title: Pytest 6: dynamically added xfail marker during test call is ignored and failure reported as FAIL
problem_statement.Description: Adding pytest.mark.xfail to a test node during the test body (via request.node.add_marker) no longer converts a subsequent failure into an xfail in pytest 6.x; instead the failure is reported as a normal FAIL. This used to work in pytest 5.x.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) The code that decides whether a test is xfailed caches the evaluated xfail result in item._store[xfailed_key]. The evaluation is performed in pytest_runtest_setup and (if missing) in pytest_runtest_call before the test body runs (file: src/_pytest/skipping.py, functions pytest_runtest_setup and pytest_runtest_call). 2) pytest_runtest_makereport reads this cached value (item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)) but does not re-evaluate xfail marks if the cached value is None (file: src/_pytest/skipping.py, function pytest_runtest_makereport). 3) Because evaluation is done prior to executing the test body, any xfail marker added during the call phase (request.node.add_marker called inside the test) is not visible to the earlier evaluation and therefore makereport sees None and does not re-evaluate — so the failure is left as a FAIL instead of being converted to XFAIL. 4) The correct relationship is: when item._store[xfailed_key] is None at makereport time, the code should call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and cache the result (item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)) so markers added during the call phase affect the final report.
StepsToReproduce: Create file test_foo.py with the following contents:
import pytest
def test_xfail_test(request):
mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason='xfail')
request.node.add_marker(mark)
assert 0
Run pytest 6.x on the file: pytest -rsx test_foo.py
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If a test adds pytest.mark.xfail to itself during the test body before a failing assertion, the final test result shown to the user should be XFAIL (treated like a statically-marked xfail), and the short test summary should list the test as XFAILED.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When running under pytest 6.x, adding an xfail marker during the test body does not affect the final outcome: the failing assertion is reported as a normal FAIL and the short test summary shows the test as failed.

problem_statement.Title: tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory fails when getpass.getuser() contains path-separator or other filesystem-illegal characters
problem_statement.Description: Creating temporary directories via pytest's tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory can fail (FileNotFoundError) when the system username returned by getpass.getuser() contains characters that are interpreted as path separators or are illegal in filenames (e.g. a domain-qualified name 'contoso\john_doe'). Pytest currently uses the raw username to build the per-user base temp directory name.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:get_user() returns the raw value of getpass.getuser() (or None on ImportError/KeyError). This assumes that the username is safe to embed directly in filesystem path components. 2) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() constructs the base directory with temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") and then calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) (mkdir called without parents=True). Because joinpath interprets path separators in the username (e.g. backslash on Windows), a username like 'contoso\john_doe' becomes nested path components 'pytest-of-contoso/john_doe'. The code both (a) embeds an unsafe/raw username into a path component and (b) relies on mkdir without parents to create that structure, causing FileNotFoundError when intermediate directories are missing. 3) The correct relationship for the username value used in path names is: it must be filesystem-safe (no path separators or illegal filename characters). The investigation specifies one suitable normalization: replace any non-word characters with underscores (regex [^\w] -> '_') so that e.g. 'contoso\\john_doe' -> 'contoso_john_doe', 'acme/joe' -> 'acme_joe', and 'john doe@example.com' -> 'john_doe_example_com'.
StepsToReproduce: Create a minimal pytest test: echo def test_tmpdir(tmpdir):>test_tmp.py && echo   pass>>test_tmp.py
From Windows cmd, set the username environment variable to a domain-qualified name and run pytest: set LOGNAME=contoso\john_doe && py.test test_tmp.py

Alternatively, on a Windows session over SSH where getpass.getuser() returns a domain-qualified name, run: python -c "import getpass; print(getpass.getuser())" to confirm it prints 'contoso\john_doe', then run a pytest that uses tmpdir_factory.mktemp('foobar') and observe failure.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should create the per-user base temporary directory and any tmpdir/tmp_path_factory temporary directories without error. The per-user directory name shown to users should be a single filesystem-safe component (e.g. pytest-of-<username>) where <username> does not contain path separators or other illegal filename characters, so directory creation succeeds and tests receive a usable temporary path.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Attempting to create a tmpdir (e.g. via tmpdir_factory.mktemp('foobar') or a test using tmpdir) raises FileNotFoundError on directory creation. Example error: FileNotFoundError: [WinError 3] The system cannot find the path specified: 'C:\Users\john_doe\AppData\Local\Temp\pytest-of-contoso\john_doe'. The username returned by getpass.getuser() (e.g. 'contoso\john_doe') is embedded verbatim into the path, causing joinpath to create nested components and mkdir (without parents) to fail.

problem_statement.Title: Misleading guidance when using pytest.skip at module level; message omits allow_module_level and no skip_module convenience API
problem_statement.Description: When pytest.skip() is called at module import time (top-level) without allow_module_level, pytest raises a CollectError with a message that discourages module-level use and suggests pytestmark. That guidance is misleading for cases where a module must be skipped before importing code that uses newer Python syntax. The error message does not mention the existing allow_module_level flag nor a convenience skip_module API, and the message text contains a formatting error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _pytest/outcomes.py defines skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False) and the Skipped exception carries e.allow_module_level. The implementation supports allow_module_level=True (and importorskip raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True)), but the default and public guidance treat module-level skipping as disallowed. (Location: src/_pytest/outcomes.py)

2) _pytest/python.py (Module._importtestmodule) catches skip.Exception and, when e.allow_module_level is False, raises a CollectError whose message: (a) forbids using pytest.skip outside tests, (b) recommends pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif} as the remedy and (c) omits any mention that pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) is a supported way to skip at module level. The message therefore encodes the incorrect assumption that module-level skip via the skip() function is not the supported/appropriate mechanism. (Location: src/_pytest/python.py, in the except skip.Exception as e block)

3) The top-level pytest namespace exports skip but does not provide a dedicated, discoverable convenience helper for module-level skipping (e.g. pytest.skip_module). This absence makes the correct allow_module_level usage less discoverable. (Location: src/pytest/__init__.py and public API surface)

4) The CollectError message text in python.py contains a formatting/typo issue (an unclosed backtick) and therefore both communicates incorrectly and is syntactically flawed as presented to users. (Location: src/_pytest/python.py string literal)

5) The message recommendation to use pytestmark implicitly assumes that marking the module prevents import/parse-time problems; that assumption is incorrect for the user scenario described (pytestmark does not prevent Python from parsing/compiling a module and therefore does not avoid syntax errors caused by newer syntax on older interpreters). This incorrect assumption is encoded in the guidance string in Module._importtestmodule. (Location: src/_pytest/python.py message content)
StepsToReproduce:
Create a test file test_pos_only.py with a module-level skip guard that does NOT set allow_module_level, for example:

from pytest import skip
import sys
if sys.version_info < (3, 8):
    skip(msg="Requires Python >= 3.8")

from pos_only import *

def test_foo():
    assert foo(10, 20) == 30
Run pytest using an interpreter older than 3.8 (e.g., Python 3.7) and observe the failure raised during collection/import.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If a user invokes a module-level skip in a test module, pytest should present clear, accurate guidance: the error should explain that skipping an entire module during import/collection is supported via pytest.skip(msg, allow_module_level=True) (and/or a discoverable convenience API such as pytest.skip_module(msg)), and it should note that using pytestmark does not avoid Python parsing/compile-time syntax errors. The message should be well-formed and not discourage the supported module-level skip usage.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When skip() is raised at module level without allow_module_level, pytest raises a CollectError with a message that states 'Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed' and recommends using pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}. The message does not mention pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) nor any module-level helper, and it contains an unclosed backtick. This leads to confusing guidance for cases where the module must be skipped before importing code that uses newer Python syntax.

Thanks for raising this.

Definitely we should improve that message. 

> Explain skip with allow_module_level in the error message. this seems in conflict with the spirit of the message.

I'm 👍 on this. 2 is also good, but because `allow_module_level` already exists and is part of the public API, I don't think introducing a new API will really help, better to improve the docs of what we already have.

Perhaps improve the message to something like this:

```
Using pytest.skip outside of a test will skip the entire module, if that's your intention pass `allow_module_level=True`. 
If you want to skip a specific test or entire class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.
```

I think we can drop the `pytestmark` remark from there, it is not skip-specific and passing `allow_module_level` already accomplishes the same.

Thanks @nicoddemus.

> Using pytest.skip outside of a test will skip the entire module, if that's your intention pass `allow_module_level=True`. 
If you want to skip a specific test or entire class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.

This sounds clearer.
Can you give a bit of context of why the message is there in the first place?
It sounds like we should be able to automatically detect if this is skipping a test or skipping the entire module (based on the fact that we can issue the warning).

Maybe this is addressing some past confusion, or we want to push people toward `pytest.mark.skip[if]`, but if we can detect it automatically - we can also deprecate allow_module_level and make `skip()` do the right thing based on the context it's used in.
> Maybe this is addressing some past confusion

That's exactly it, people would use `@pytest.skip` instead of `@pytest.mark.skip` and skip the whole module:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/2338#issuecomment-290324255

For that reason we don't really want to automatically detect things, but want users to explicitly pass that flag which proves they are not doing it by accident.

Original issue: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/607
Having looked at the links, I think the alternative API to skip a module is more appealing.
Here is a proposed end state:

1. pytest.skip_module is introduced, can be used to skip a module.
2. pytest.skip() is only legal inside of a test. If called outside of a test, an error message is issues.
Example:

> pytest.skip should only be used inside tests. To skip a module use pytest.skip_module. To completely skip a test function or a test class, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators.

Getting to this end state would include deprecating allow_module_level first, directing people using pytest.skip(allow_module_level=True) to use pytest.skip_module().

I am also fine with just changing the message as you initially proposed but I feel this proposal will result in an healthier state.

problem_statement.Title: Assertion failure shows extra decorator line in failure context on Python 3.9
problem_statement.Description: When an assertion fails, pytest prints an extra subsequent physical line (the '@t' decorator) as part of the assertion context in the failure output on Python 3.9. The same test output on Python 3.7 does not include that extra line.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _get_assertion_exprs in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py assumes only tokenize.NEWLINE and tokenize.ENDMARKER terminate an assertion expression. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py::_get_assertion_exprs. Incorrect assumption: tokenize.NL cannot represent an end-of-assert physical newline. Evidence: on Python 3.9 the tokenizer can emit tokenize.NL for the physical newline that precedes a decorator, so NL is currently ignored and the following decorator line is collected as part of the assertion expression. 

2) The correct termination relationship (and the precise diagnostic) is: treat NEWLINE and ENDMARKER as terminators, and also treat tokenize.NL as a terminator when not nested inside parentheses/brackets/braces (i.e. when depth == 0). Expressed as the exact conditional used to decide termination: 

    tp in {tokenize.NEWLINE, tokenize.ENDMARKER} or (tp == tokenize.NL and depth == 0)

This relationship must be applied in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py::_get_assertion_exprs. 

3) Implicit version-stability assumption: the code assumes tokenization semantics are identical across Python versions. Location: same function. Evidence: behavior differs between Python 3.7 and 3.9, causing the mis-attribution of subsequent lines into assertion expressions.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an assert fails, the failure output shows only the assert statement and the lines that are part of its logical expression. It should not include unrelated subsequent physical lines such as a following decorator.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On Python 3.9 the assertion failure output includes an extra physical line (the '@t' decorator) in the assertion context, i.e. the decorator line is printed between the assertion and the assertion diff.
